title: parseInt
explanation: parseInt parses up to the first non-numeric character.

title: Array length
explanation: Setting an array's length to 0 empties the array.

title: delete operator
explanation: delete removes the element but does not reindex the array, leaving an empty slot.

title: Object keys
explanation: Object keys are always converted to strings.

title: Adding to arrays
explanation: Assigning to an index out of bounds expands the array, filling intermediate slots with empty items.

title: parseFloat
explanation: parseFloat parses up to the first invalid character for a float.

title: Math.max
explanation: With no arguments, Math.max returns -Infinity.

title: Math.min
explanation: With no arguments, Math.min returns Infinity.

title: Const object mutation
explanation: const prevents reassignment of the variable, but does not make the object immutable.

title: String index
explanation: Strings can be accessed by index like arrays.

title: Double NOT
explanation: Non-empty strings are truthy. !! converts it to a boolean.

title: Array push
explanation: Array.prototype.push returns the new length of the array.

title: typeof function
explanation: Functions have their own specific typeof result, even though they are objects.

title: typeof NaN
explanation: NaN stands for Not-a-Number, but its type is technically 'number'.

title: delete property
explanation: The delete operator returns true if the property was successfully deleted.

title: in operator
explanation: The 'in' operator checks if an index/key exists in the object/array.

title: Spread operator
explanation: The spread operator expands an iterable (like a string) into an array.

title: Closure basics
explanation: The inner arrow function forms a closure over 'i', preserving its state.

title: Arrow function this
explanation: Arrow functions do not have their own 'this'. They inherit it from the enclosing lexical scope (global object in this case).

title: setTimeout let
explanation: Using 'let' creates a new block scope for each iteration, preserving the value of 'i'.

title: setTimeout var
explanation: Using 'var' binds 'i' to the function scope. By the time setTimeout runs, 'i' is 3.

title: Object coercion
explanation: The object is converted to '[object Object]' and the array to an empty string.

title: Array comparison
explanation: Arrays are objects. Objects are compared by reference, not by value.

title: typeof null and undefined
explanation: typeof null is 'object', typeof undefined is 'undefined'.

title: bind method
explanation: bind creates a new function with 'this' permanently bound to the provided object.

title: call method
explanation: call invokes the function immediately with the provided 'this' value and arguments.

title: IIFE scope
explanation: b = 3 creates a global variable 'b'. 'a' is locally scoped to the IIFE.

title: Default arguments
code:
function f(a = 1, b = 2) { return a + b; }
console.log(f(undefined, null));
explanation: Default parameters only trigger on undefined, not null. So a becomes 1, b remains null. 1 + null -> 1.

title: Arguments object
explanation: In non-strict mode, the arguments object is linked to named parameters.

title: Array map with parseInt
explanation: map passes (value, index) to parseInt. parseInt('7', 1) is NaN, parseInt('11', 2) is 3.

title: Object properties
explanation: Object keys are stringified. Both 'b' and 'c' become '[object Object]'. Thus a['[object Object]'] is overwritten.

title: typeof undeclared
explanation: typeof is a safe operator that returns 'undefined' for undeclared variables instead of throwing an error.

title: Destructuring
explanation: Destructuring extracts 'a' into variable 'x'. Since 'a' is provided (2), the default (1) is ignored.

title: Rest parameters
code:
function f(...args) { return typeof args; }
console.log(f(1, 2));
explanation: Rest parameters collect arguments into an array, and typeof an array is 'object'.

title: Promise executor
explanation: The Promise executor function runs synchronously.

title: Microtasks
explanation: .then() callbacks are pushed to the microtask queue and run after synchronous code completes.

title: Object assign
explanation: Object.assign mutates the target object and returns it, so they refer to the same object.

title: String slice
explanation: Negative indices in slice count backwards from the end of the string.

title: Array sort
explanation: Array.prototype.sort converts elements to strings and compares their UTF-16 code units.

title: Array reduce
explanation: The initial value is an empty string, so the numbers are concatenated as strings.

title: Nullish coalescing
explanation: Nullish coalescing (??) only falls back if the left operand is null or undefined, not 0 or false.

title: Optional chaining
explanation: Optional chaining returns undefined if the target is null or undefined without throwing an error.

title: Number precision
explanation: Floating-point math in JS uses IEEE 754, resulting in 0.30000000000000004.

title: Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER
explanation: Numbers beyond Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER lose precision, making them evaluate as equal.

title: String replace
explanation: String.prototype.replace only replaces the first occurrence unless a global regex is used.

title: Function length
explanation: A function's length is the number of parameters before the first one with a default value.

title: eval scope
code:
function f() { eval('var a = 1'); return a; }
console.log(f());
explanation: eval in non-strict mode modifies the local scope, declaring 'a'.

title: this in classes
explanation: Class bodies are implicitly in strict mode, so 'this' does not default to the global object.

title: Object freeze
explanation: Object.freeze is shallow; it doesn't freeze nested objects or arrays.

title: Symbol equality
explanation: Every Symbol() call creates a completely unique symbol.

title: Object spread
explanation: Spread properties to the right overwrite properties with the same key to the left.

title: Set duplicate
explanation: Sets use same-value-zero equality. 1 and '1' are different types.

title: Map key types
explanation: Maps check keys by reference. {} creates a new object reference.

title: Proxy traps
code:
let p = new Proxy({}, { get: () => 42 });
console.log(p.x);
explanation: The Proxy 'get' trap intercepts property access, returning 42 for any property.

title: yield*
explanation: yield* delegates to another iterable object.

title: in operator vs undefined
explanation: The index 0 exists in the array and holds the value undefined.

title: Delete array element
explanation: delete removes the element entirely, making 'in' return false for that index.

title: Promise.all fail-fast
explanation: Promise.all rejects immediately if any of the provided promises reject.

title: Async/await sequence
explanation: await converts non-promises into resolved promises.

title: Event loop ordering
explanation: Synchronous runs first (3), then microtasks (2), then macrotasks (1).

title: Reflect API
explanation: Reflect API methods like deleteProperty return a boolean indicating success.

title: WeakMap GC
explanation: WeakMaps allow keys to be garbage collected if there are no other references to them.

title: Generator state
explanation: After yielding 1, the generator is finished on the next call, so done becomes true.

title: Tagged template
explanation: Tagged template literals pass string parts as the first argument, and evaluated expressions as the rest.

title: Array.from mapping
explanation: Array.from can take a map function as a second argument, populating the new array.

title: Custom iterables
explanation: Objects can become iterable by defining a Symbol.iterator method.

title: try/catch/finally
explanation: A return statement in the finally block overwrites any return in the try block.

title: Throwing null
explanation: You can throw anything in JavaScript, including null.

title: BigInt type
explanation: Appending 'n' creates a BigInt primitive.

title: BigInt mixing
explanation: You cannot implicitly mix BigInt and Number types in mathematical operations.

title: Static properties
explanation: Static properties are inherited by subclasses.

title: Private fields
code:
class A { #x = 1; getX() { return this.#x; } }
console.log(new A().getX());
explanation: Private fields (#x) are accessible only within the class body.

title: new.target
code:
function F() { console.log(new.target === F); }
new F();
explanation: new.target refers to the constructor that was directly invoked by 'new'.

title: Dynamic import
explanation: Dynamic import() returns a Promise that resolves to a module namespace object.

title: Array flat
explanation: flat(Infinity) flattens nested arrays of arbitrary depth.

title: Nullish coalescing vs OR
explanation: || falls back on falsy values (like ''). ?? falls back only on nullish values.

title: String matchAll
explanation: matchAll returns an iterator of all regex matches.

title: Object.fromEntries
explanation: Object.fromEntries transforms a list of key-value pairs into an object.

title: Intl.NumberFormat
explanation: The Intl API provides powerful language-sensitive formatting.

title: Promise.any
explanation: Promise.any resolves as soon as any promise fulfills, ignoring rejections.

title: Top-level await
explanation: In ES modules, await can be used outside of async functions.

title: Logical assignment
explanation: a ||= 1 assigns 1 to a only if a is falsy (0 is falsy).

title: Nullish assignment
explanation: a ??= 1 assigns 1 to a only if a is null or undefined.

title: String replaceAll
explanation: replaceAll replaces all occurrences without needing a global regex.

title: Array at
explanation: The at() method supports negative indexing.

title: Object.hasOwn
explanation: Object.hasOwn is a safer alternative to obj.hasOwnProperty('a').

title: structuredClone
explanation: structuredClone performs a deep copy, so nested references are different.
